title: Effects of surface viscosities on the motion of a droplet enclosing a translating particle
authors: Ali Gürbüz, Hervé Nganguia, Guangpu Zhu, Lailai Zhu, Y. N. Young, On Shun Pak
abstract: We investigate the influence of interfacial rheology on the motion of a compound particle consisting of a viscous droplet enclosing a translating rigid particle in the Stokes flow regime. The droplet interface is modeled using the Boussinesq-Scriven constitutive law, incorporating both surface shear and dilatational viscosities. An exact analytical solution is derived for the concentric configuration, and the analysis is extended to eccentric geometries using a spectral boundary integral method, enabling a systematic examination of confinement, viscosity contrast, and interfacial properties. For concentric configurations, we show that the induced droplet velocity is independent of surface shear viscosity, while surface dilatational viscosity can either enhance or suppress the droplet motion depending on the interplay between confinement and viscosity ratio. This behavior is rationalized in terms of competing effects between reduced interfacial mobility and increased driving force required to maintain the prescribed particle speed. In contrast, when the particle is eccentrically positioned within the droplet, a dependence on surface shear viscosity emerges, leading to a consistent enhancement of droplet motion that becomes more pronounced with increasing eccentricity. The analytical and numerical results are in excellent agreement and reveal how interfacial rheology, confinement, and symmetry breaking jointly govern the dynamics of compound particle systems. These findings provide mechanistic insight and establish a quantitative benchmark for future studies of active compound particles with complex interfaces.
summary: This paper studies how the surface properties of a droplet, specifically its shear and dilatational viscosities, influence its movement when it contains a moving internal particle. It shows that the symmetry of the particle's position (concentric vs. eccentric) fundamentally changes how surface rheology affects the system's dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating how interfacial rheology (surface shear and dilatational viscosities) affects the motion of a viscous droplet containing a translating rigid particle.
main_result: In concentric configurations, droplet velocity is independent of surface shear viscosity but depends on surface dilatational viscosity; in eccentric configurations, surface shear viscosity enhances droplet motion.
method: Derivation of an exact analytical solution for concentric geometries and use of a spectral boundary integral method for eccentric geometries.
model_or_system: A compound particle consisting of a viscous droplet enclosing a translating rigid particle in the Stokes flow regime, with the interface modeled using the Boussinesq-Scriven constitutive law.
important_parameters: Surface shear viscosity, surface dilatational viscosity, viscosity contrast, confinement, and particle eccentricity.
main_assumptions: Stokes flow regime (low Reynolds number) and Boussinesq-Scriven constitutive law for the interface.
paper_structure: The paper presents an analytical solution for concentric configurations, extends the analysis to eccentric geometries via numerical methods, and provides a mechanistic rationalization of the observed velocity dependencies.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
